answer.summary: AIR 1973 SC 1461 is the reported citation for Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala decided by the Supreme Court of India on 24 April 1973. The petitions raised constitutional questions about the validity of the Twenty‑fourth, Twenty‑fifth and Twenty‑ninth Amendments, interpretation of Article 368 (including its pre‑amendment scope), and enforcement of fundamental rights under Articles 14, 19, 25, 26 and 31; the judgment was structured into eight parts addressing those issues. The supplied evidence is a partial excerpt (paras 1–9) outlining the case parties, the reliefs sought in Writ Petition No. 135 of 1970, and the topics the Court proposed to examine; it does not include the Court’s full reasoning or final holdings, so no further conclusions beyond these procedural and subject‑matter points can be drawn from this excerpt.

answer.summary: 1993 AIR(SCW) 4101 appears in the provided evidence as the Supreme Court matter Supreme Court Advocate-on-Record Association v. Union of India (decision dated 6 October 1993). The recorded excerpts (paras 5–7) show that the issues included justiciability (including fixation of High Court judge-strength under Article 216) and that senior counsel urged reconsideration of the S.P. Gupta majority, arguing for the primacy of the Chief Justice of India in judicial appointments and transfers, with the executive confined to giving effect to the CJI’s opinion except perhaps in very exceptional disclosed circumstances. The evidence here is partial (only paras 5–7 are shown), so I cannot provide the full holding or other details beyond what is quoted.

answer.summary: The newest murder-related Gujarat case visible in the supplied results is **Raval Shaileshbhai Rameshbhai Virchandbhai v. State of Gujarat**, decided by the **High Court of Gujarat (D.B.) on 17 February 2026**. From the extracted paragraphs, it concerns a fatal knife assault that led to addition of **Section 302 IPC** after the injured person died during treatment. The next most recent is **Pintubhai @ Kalio Dolubhai Vasava v. State of Gujarat**, decided on **12 February 2026**, also by the **High Court of Gujarat (D.B.)**, involving conviction under **Sections 302/114 IPC** in a case arising from harassment and the killing of Parvatiben. An older but still recent result in the supplied evidence is **Raju @ Rajubhai Haribhai Gohel v. State of Gujarat**, decided on **09 December 2025** by the same court, relating to a Rajkot murder prosecution involving stabbing during a dispute. All the visible authorities are from the **High Court of Gujarat, Division Bench**, so there is no mixed-jurisdiction issue in this set.

answer.summary: In RAVAL SHAILESHBHAI RAMESHBHAI VIRCHANDBHAI v. State of Gujarat, 2026 LawSuit(Guj) 327, the Gujarat High Court (Division Bench) was dealing with a murder case arising from a quarrel that allegedly turned violent very quickly. From the extract provided, the prosecution case was that the accused used highly abusive language about the deceased’s mother and sister, and when the deceased objected and did not escalate the exchange, the accused allegedly lost self-control, opened a folding knife, and delivered repeated blows on vital parts of the body, including the stomach, chest, abdomen, shoulder, hand, and neck. The complainant, described as an eyewitness and injured person, was also hurt while trying to intervene. The legal point visible from this extract is the prosecution’s reliance on repeated knife blows on vital parts to show a specific intention to cause death, supporting a murder charge. The practical takeaway is that even if the incident begins as a sudden quarrel, the nature of the weapon, the number of blows, and the targeting of vital organs can be treated as strong indicators of homicidal intent. The final ruling or outcome is not fully visible in the supplied evidence.

answer.summary: AIR 1973 SC 1461 corresponds to the case Kesavananda Bharati Sripadagalvaru v. State of Kerala (petitions including Raghunath Rao Ganpat Rao, Shethia Mining & Mfg. Co. Ltd., Oriental Coal Co. Ltd. v. State of Kerala & Union of India), decided by the Supreme Court on 24 April 1973. The supplied evidence shows the parties, court and decision date and that the judgment is divided into multiple parts addressing the Twenty-fourth, Twenty-fifth and Twenty-ninth Constitutional Amendment Acts and related constitutional questions. The evidence provided is partial and does not set out the Court’s full holdings or the commonly referenced “basic structure” formulation in detail. For the full judgment text, holdings, and subsequent legal effects, consult the complete reported decision or authoritative law reports.
